question: Earth, Wind & Fire's "September" has a famous nonsense lyric the songwriter begged to change. What's the made-up word they sing?
hint1: It's not a real word — pure filler.
hint2: It comes right before "never was a cloudy day."
funFact: Lyricist Allee Willis begged Maurice White to swap "ba-dee-ya" for real words. He refused, teaching her: never let the lyric get in the way of the groove.

question: Michael Jackson's 14-minute "Thriller" music video was directed by a guy famous for a werewolf movie. Who?
hint1: He directed a horror-comedy about a werewolf.
funFact: The video cost around $500,000 — huge for 1983 — and is preserved in the U.S. National Film Registry.

question: The Bee Gees wrote a #1 country duet for Kenny Rogers and Dolly Parton. Name the song.
hint2: Named after an Ernest Hemingway novel.
funFact: Barry Gibb said the song was originally written in an R&B style, intended for Marvin Gaye.

question: On his 1978 debut album "For You," Prince played essentially every instrument himself. Roughly how many did the credits list?
hint2: He wrote, produced, arranged, and performed all of it — at age 19.
funFact: The feat drew comparisons to Stevie Wonder, who also famously played nearly everything on his records.

question: What 1979 Sugarhill Gang track was hip hop's first Top 40 hit, built on the groove of a Chic song?
funFact: Nile Rodgers threatened to sue over the borrowed groove before getting a songwriting credit.

question: Queen's "Bohemian Rhapsody" has no chorus and a wild mid-section in what theatrical vocal style?
funFact: They overdubbed vocals so many times the tape reportedly wore thin enough to see through.

question: James Brown, the "Godfather of Soul," is credited with pioneering the rhythmic foundation of which genre by emphasizing "the One"?
hint2: Heavy emphasis on the first beat.
funFact: Shifting emphasis to the downbeat ("the One") became the rhythmic backbone of funk and later hip hop.

question: Whitney Houston's massive hit "I Will Always Love You" was originally written and recorded by which country legend?
hint1: She wrote it as a professional goodbye.
hint2: Country icon, theme park named after her.
funFact: Dolly wrote it in 1973 about parting ways with her mentor Porter Wagoner.

question: The dense, layered "Wall of Sound" production style was pioneered by which famous — and later infamous — producer?
funFact: He had many musicians play the same parts at once to create an orchestral, almost overwhelming pop sound.

question: The Roland TR-808 drum machine defined '80s hip hop. Today "808" usually refers to which specific sound?
hint2: Rappers say "the 808s" meaning the booming bass.
funFact: Kanye West named an entire album "808s & Heartbreak" after the machine.

question: Which Detroit record label was home to The Supremes, Marvin Gaye, and Stevie Wonder?
funFact: "Motown" blends "motor" and "town" — a nod to Detroit's car industry.

question: An 1896 war between Britain and Zanzibar holds a strange record. For what?
hint1: It's about how long it lasted.
funFact: The Anglo-Zanzibar War lasted roughly 38 minutes — the shortest recorded war ever.

question: In 1518 Strasbourg, a bizarre "plague" struck where people did what uncontrollably for days?
funFact: Dozens danced for days during the "Dancing Plague"; theories range from mass hysteria to ergot poisoning.

question: Which ancient Wonder — a giant bronze harbor statue — was toppled by an earthquake and later sold for scrap?
hint1: A massive statue at a harbor.
hint2: On a Greek island in the Aegean.
funFact: It stood about 33 meters, fell around 226 BC, and its bronze was eventually sold off centuries later.

question: Which South American empire kept records using knotted strings called "quipu" — with no written alphabet?
funFact: Quipu encoded numbers and possibly stories in knots; much of it still isn't fully deciphered.

question: In 1932, which country's military famously "lost" the Great Emu War to flightless birds?
funFact: Soldiers with machine guns couldn't cull the emus, which scattered and outmaneuvered them.

question: Cleopatra lived closer in time to which event: the building of the Great Pyramid, or the Moon landing?
hint1: The pyramids were already ancient to her.
funFact: The Great Pyramid was built ~2560 BC, Cleopatra died 30 BC, and the Moon landing was 1969 — she's closer to us than to the pyramids.

question: Oxford University was already teaching students before which empire — destroyed in 1521 — even founded its capital?
funFact: Oxford had teaching by 1096; the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan wasn't founded until 1325.

question: Which country sold Alaska to the United States in 1867 in a deal mocked as "Seward's Folly"?
hint2: It's just across the Bering Strait.
funFact: The U.S. paid about $7.2 million — roughly 2 cents an acre — and critics laughed until gold and oil turned up.

question: The French Revolution introduced a new calendar with how many days per week instead of seven?
funFact: The Republican Calendar had 10-day weeks and even decimal clocks — it didn't catch on.

question: What soft drink was first sold in the 1880s by a pharmacist as a "brain tonic" and headache cure?
funFact: Early versions actually contained coca leaf extract — hence the name.

question: Under the Mongol Empire's "Pax Mongolica," legend says a person carrying what could travel the empire unharmed?
hint2: The point was how safe travel and trade became.
funFact: Whether literally true or not, the Mongols did make the Silk Road safe enough that trade boomed.

question: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh roughly how much on Earth?
funFact: Neutron stars are so dense a sugar-cube-sized piece weighs about as much as a mountain.

question: How many hearts does an octopus have?
hint2: Two serve the gills, one serves the body.
funFact: Their blood is blue because it's copper-based, not iron-based like ours.

question: Bananas are slightly radioactive thanks to a natural isotope of which element?
funFact: Scientists jokingly measure tiny radiation doses in "banana equivalent doses."

question: Honey basically never spoils. Archaeologists found still-edible honey in tombs about how old?
funFact: Honey's low moisture, acidity, and natural hydrogen peroxide make it nearly impossible for bacteria to grow.

question: Wombats produce poop in what unusual shape that won't roll away?
hint2: It stacks without rolling off rocks.
funFact: Their intestines shape the cubes — researchers won an Ig Nobel Prize figuring out how.

question: On which planet is a single day longer than its entire year?
hint2: It spins extremely slowly — and backwards.
funFact: A Venusian day (~243 Earth days) is longer than its year (~225 Earth days).

question: Sharks are older than which woody, leafy thing on Earth?
funFact: Sharks predate not just trees but also dinosaurs and even Saturn's rings.

question: The Eiffel Tower can grow taller in summer by about how much, thanks to heat?
funFact: The iron structure expands in summer heat, adding up to ~15 cm to its height.

question: Under the right conditions hot water can freeze faster than cold water. What's this counterintuitive effect called?
hint2: A Tanzanian schoolboy noticed it making ice cream.
funFact: Erasto Mpemba spotted it in a school cooking class; scientists still debate exactly why it happens.

question: Roughly how many times per second does lightning strike somewhere on Earth?
funFact: That's around 8 million lightning strikes every single day across the planet.

question: Humans share roughly what percentage of their DNA with a banana?
funFact: We share basic cell-machinery genes with nearly all life, which is why even a banana overlaps about half.

question: By raw cell count, your body has more of what than human cells?
hint2: Most of them live in your gut.
funFact: Estimates suggest roughly as many — or slightly more — bacterial cells than human cells in and on you.

question: What's the longest a person has verifiably gone without sleep, set by a teenager in 1964?
hint2: Randy Gardner, for a science fair.
funFact: Guinness later stopped recognizing sleep-deprivation records because they're dangerous.

question: The hottest pepper ever certified by Guinness, created by Ed Currie in 2023, has a one-letter name. What is it?
funFact: Pepper X averages over 2.6 million Scoville units; the Carolina Reaper held the record before it.

question: The tallest verified human in history, Robert Wadlow, reached about how tall?
funFact: His extreme height came from a pituitary condition; he was still growing when he died at 22.

question: The loudest sound in recorded history was an 1883 volcanic eruption heard ~3,000 miles away. Which volcano?
funFact: The blast ruptured the eardrums of sailors 40 miles away and circled the globe several times as a pressure wave.

question: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep, lies in which trench?
funFact: It's deeper than Mount Everest is tall, and only a handful of people have ever reached the bottom.

question: The fastest humans have ever traveled was aboard which spacecraft, at about 24,800 mph?
hint2: The dress rehearsal right before Apollo 11.
funFact: Apollo 10's crew hit ~39,900 km/h on the way back to Earth — still the human speed record.

question: The most expensive spice in the world by weight, hand-picked from a flower, is what?
hint2: Each crocus flower gives only a few.
funFact: It takes about 150,000 flowers to make a single kilogram, all harvested by hand.

question: The longest continuous game of Monopoly on record lasted over how long?
funFact: Guinness lists the longest Monopoly marathon at 70 straight days.

question: The largest living structure on Earth, visible from space, is what?
funFact: It stretches about 2,300 km and is built by billions of tiny coral polyps.

question: What's the fastest land animal, hitting around 70 mph in short bursts?
funFact: A cheetah accelerates faster than most supercars but can only hold top speed for a few seconds.

question: With pre-breathing pure oxygen, the static-apnea breath-hold record exceeds how long underwater?
hint2: Free divers load up on oxygen first.
funFact: Even without oxygen assistance, elite free divers can hold their breath past 11 minutes.

question: In "One Piece," what's the name of the legendary treasure Luffy is hunting for?
hint2: Left behind by the Pirate King, Gol D. Roger.
funFact: Creator Eiichiro Oda has known the ending since the very beginning and insists the One Piece is a real, physical treasure.

question: In "Naruto," what's the name of the Nine-Tailed Fox sealed inside Naruto?
funFact: Kurama is the strongest of the nine Tailed Beasts, and its true name isn't revealed until late in the series.

question: "Dragon Ball" is loosely inspired by which classic Chinese novel about a Monkey King?
hint2: A monkey with a magic staff and flying cloud.
funFact: Goku's early design — tail, power pole, and flying Nimbus cloud — comes straight from Sun Wukong.

question: In "Attack on Titan," the three walls protecting humanity are Maria, Rose, and what?
funFact: From outer to inner they go Maria, Rose, then Sina — where the wealthy and royalty live.

question: In "Demon Slayer," which breathing style does Tanjiro primarily use early in the series?
hint2: He later shifts to a fire/sun style.
funFact: Tanjiro eventually masters Sun Breathing (Hinokami Kagura), the original style all others descend from.

question: In "Jujutsu Kaisen," what does Yuji swallow that makes him the vessel for a powerful curse?
hint2: There are 20 of them scattered around.
funFact: Sukuna's 20 preserved fingers are cursed objects; eating one makes Yuji his living host.

question: In "Fullmetal Alchemist," what's the core law stating you must give something of equal value to gain something?
hint2: Equal value in, equal value out.
funFact: The whole story springs from the Elric brothers breaking this law trying to bring back their mother.

question: Studio Ghibli's logo is a big fuzzy forest spirit from which film?
funFact: Totoro became the studio's mascot and one of the most recognizable characters in all of anime.

question: In "My Hero Academia," what are superpowers commonly called?
hint1: One word — a synonym for a personality trait.
funFact: The hero Deku is born Quirkless before inheriting the power "One For All."

question: "Pokémon" was inspired by the creator's real childhood hobby of collecting what?
hint2: Satoshi Tajiri did it as a kid.
funFact: Tajiri's love of catching and collecting bugs became the whole catch-'em-all concept.

question: In "Bleach," the soul-cutting swords carried by Soul Reapers are called what?
hint2: Each one has its own name and spirit.
funFact: Every Zanpakuto has release forms — "shikai" and the much stronger "bankai."

question: In "Death Note," the notebook kills anyone whose name is written in it — but you also have to do what?
hint1: Something you must know about them.
hint2: Otherwise people with the same name would all die.
funFact: The Shinigami Ryuk drops the notebook into the human world simply because he was bored.

question: Which NBA player holds the record for most points in a single quarter, with 37?
funFact: He hit 13 of 13 shots in that third quarter against the Kings in 2015 — 9 of them threes.

question: Who holds the NBA record for most points in a single game, with 100?
hint2: He did it in 1962 against the Knicks.
funFact: There's no video footage of the game — only radio audio and a famous photo of Wilt holding a "100" sign.

question: Which is the only NFL team to finish a season perfect, including winning the Super Bowl?
funFact: They went 17–0, and surviving players reportedly toast each year when the last unbeaten team finally loses.

question: What is Usain Bolt's 100-meter world record time, to two decimals?
funFact: He reached a top speed near 27.8 mph during that race — and looked relaxed doing it.

question: Which player has won the most Ballon d'Or awards in soccer history?
hint2: He's won it a record number of times.
funFact: Messi has collected more Ballon d'Or trophies than any other player in the award's history.

question: Diego Maradona's infamous illegal "Hand of God" goal came in the 1986 World Cup against which country?
hint2: He punched it in with his hand.
funFact: Minutes later he scored the "Goal of the Century," dribbling past half the England team.

question: In baseball, hitting a single, double, triple, and home run all in one game is called what?
hint2: All four hit types in one game.
funFact: The triple is usually the hardest piece — making the cycle rarer than a no-hitter in some seasons.

question: Which country has won the most men's FIFA World Cups, with five?
hint2: Their kit is yellow and green.
funFact: Brazil is also the only nation to have played in every single World Cup tournament.

question: Modern gymnastics has multiple elements literally named "the Biles" after which athlete?
funFact: She has so many signature skills that judges have sometimes been accused of under-scoring them to discourage copycats.

question: The fastest serve in tennis history, hit by Sam Groth, was about how fast?
funFact: Groth's 2012 serve is the fastest ever recorded, though it came at a smaller event.

question: In the NHL, each player on the championship team gets a unique personal tradition with the Stanley Cup. What is it?
hint2: One day each, take it home.
funFact: Players have used it as a cereal bowl, a dog bowl, and even a baptismal font.

question: Which boxer called himself "The Greatest" and lit the Olympic torch at the 1996 Atlanta Games?
funFact: His hands trembled from Parkinson's as he lit the flame — one of the most moving moments in Olympic history.

question: What 1997 film held the title of highest-grossing movie for over a decade and features an "I'm flying" scene on a ship?
funFact: Cameron eventually beat his own record with "Avatar" in 2009.

question: In "Friends," what's the name of Ross's pet monkey?
funFact: The monkey was reportedly a handful on set, and Marcel got written out fairly quickly.

question: Which acclaimed show about a chemistry-teacher-turned-drug-kingpin is set in Albuquerque?
funFact: New Mexico's film incentives helped land it in Albuquerque, which now runs Breaking Bad tours.

question: The viral 2015 "Dress" split the internet between which two color combos?
hint2: The dress is actually blue and black.
funFact: The illusion comes from how each person's brain assumes the lighting in the photo.

question: What 2013 Disney film features the song "Let It Go" and an ice queen named Elsa?
funFact: "Let It Go" was so powerful the writers reworked Elsa from a planned villain into a hero.

question: The "Wilhelm Scream" is a famous reused sound effect of what?
hint2: It's snuck into Star Wars, Indiana Jones, and hundreds more.
funFact: It dates back to a 1951 film and has become a long-running inside joke among sound editors.

question: What 2016 augmented-reality mobile game had people walking everywhere catching virtual creatures?
hint2: Gotta catch 'em all — in real life.
funFact: It briefly spiked Nintendo's market value by billions and got players to walk record distances.

question: In NBC's "The Office," what's the title of Michael Scott's homemade spy movie?
funFact: The crew filmed bits of it over several years to make the home-movie look feel authentic.

question: What blocky sandbox game is the best-selling video game of all time?
funFact: It has sold over 300 million copies — more than any other game ever made.

question: Which 1984 album made Madonna the "Queen of Pop," with a title track she performed in a wedding dress at the VMAs?
funFact: Her rolling-around-in-a-wedding-dress VMA performance became one of the most talked-about TV moments of the decade.

question: Which sitcom set around a New York coffee shop ran 10 seasons and ended in 2004?
funFact: The main cast negotiated together to each earn $1 million per episode by the final seasons.

question: What compound makes chili peppers feel hot?
hint2: It binds to your heat receptors.
funFact: Capsaicin tricks your nerves into sensing heat even though nothing's actually burning you.

question: Carrots were originally what color, long before the orange ones?
hint1: Not orange — and also white and yellow existed.
funFact: Orange carrots were popularized in the Netherlands, possibly as a nod to the House of Orange.

question: What gives Swiss cheese its holes?
hint2: Bacteria release it as the cheese ages.
funFact: The holes are actually called "eyes," and Swiss cheese with none is called "blind."

question: Most "wasabi" served outside Japan is secretly dyed-up what?
funFact: Real wasabi is hard to grow and loses its kick within minutes of being grated.

question: Chocolate is toxic to dogs because of which compound found in cocoa?
hint2: Dogs break it down very slowly.
funFact: Dark and baking chocolate carry the most theobromine, making them the most dangerous to dogs.

question: A peanut isn't actually a nut. Botanically, what is it?
hint1: Same family as beans and peas.
funFact: Peanuts develop in pods beneath the soil, unlike true tree nuts.

question: The browning of seared meat, toast, and fudge crust comes mostly from which chemical reaction?
funFact: It's different from plain caramelization and creates hundreds of new flavor and aroma compounds.

question: Why do onions make you cry — they release a gas that forms what on contact with your eyes?
funFact: Chilling the onion first or cutting it under running water cuts down the airborne sulfur compounds.

question: Honey is made by bees regurgitating and then dehydrating what?
hint2: They fan it with their wings to dry it out.
funFact: A single worker bee makes only about a twelfth of a teaspoon of honey in its whole life.

question: What spice — the priciest in the world — comes from the dried red stigmas of a crocus flower?
hint2: Each flower yields only about three.
funFact: Gram for gram it can cost more than gold, since it's all hand-harvested.

question: Sixers superstar Joel Embiid won the 2023 NBA MVP — wild, since he didn't even start playing basketball until age 15. What country was he born in?
hint2: He grew up there before moving to the U.S. for school.
funFact: From picking up a basketball at 15 to MVP by 29 — one of the wildest come-ups in NBA history. 🐐

question: The Philadelphia Eagles have one of the NFL's oldest fight songs. What three words does every Philly fan scream during it?
funFact: E-A-G-L-E-S, Eagles! 🦅 The whole kitchen sings it.

question: What's the name of the Phillies' fuzzy green mascot, one of the most famous in all of sports?
funFact: The Phanatic debuted in 1978 and is in the Mascot Hall of Fame. 🟢🔔

question: The Flyers introduced a wild orange mascot in 2018 who instantly became an internet legend. What's his name?
hint2: A one-word name that sounds tough.
funFact: Gritty became a meme overnight and Philly adopted him as a folk hero. 🟠

question: In One Piece, who is Luffy's very first crewmate — the green-haired swordsman who fights with three swords at once?
hint1: His style is called Santoryu (three-sword style).
hint2: He's famous for getting hopelessly lost.
funFact: Zoro's terrible sense of direction is a running gag the whole series — he gets lost walking in a straight line. 🗡️

question: Sixers legend Allen Iverson stood just 6'0" yet won an NBA MVP and multiple scoring titles. What one-word nickname did 'A.I.' go by?
hint1: It's also what you give to a question.
funFact: Allen Iverson — 'The Answer' — one of the most beloved Sixers ever, undersized and fearless. 🏀

question: The Eagles' near-unstoppable short-yardage QB sneak, where Jalen Hurts gets shoved forward by his teammates, has a cheeky nickname — the 'tush ___'?
funFact: Borderline unfair. P.S. Alex is so deep in his Jalen Hurts era he'd run the tush push down the aisle. 👀💍

question: Considered the greatest Phillie ever, this third baseman hit 548 career home runs and won 3 NL MVPs in red pinstripes. Who is he?
hint1: A Hall of Fame third baseman.
funFact: Mike Schmidt — 548 homers, 3 MVPs, the greatest Phillie of all time. 🔔

question: The Flyers' rough-and-tumble 'Broad Street Bullies' won back-to-back Stanley Cups in 1974 and 1975. Which gap-toothed Hall of Fame captain led them?
hint1: A fierce two-way center who later became the Flyers' GM.
hint2: Famous for his missing front teeth.
funFact: Bobby Clarke captained both Cup teams — still the only Stanley Cups in Flyers history. 🟠

question: Before the 76ers swept to the 1983 NBA title, center Moses Malone made a famous playoff prediction. What three-word phrase did he say?
hint1: He was predicting three straight series sweeps.
hint2: Three of the same word — his way of saying 'four.'
funFact: They nearly delivered, going 12-1 in the playoffs — one of the most dominant title runs ever. 🏀

question: Who is the Philadelphia 76ers' all-time leading scorer, a Hall of Fame guard from the 1960s?
hint1: His No. 15 hangs in the rafters.
hint2: A silky mid-range jump-shooter on the 1967 title team.
funFact: Hal Greer poured in 21,586 points for the franchise — still the most in Sixers history.

question: Allen Iverson's infamous 2002 Sixers press conference rant repeated which single word over and over?
hint1: He couldn't believe that's what they were talking about.
funFact: A.I. said it more than 20 times — one of the most quoted sports pressers in history. 🗣️

question: Eagles backup QB Nick Foles won Super Bowl LII MVP — and famously also CAUGHT a touchdown on which trick play?
hint1: A direct snap to the running back, who pitched it around.
hint2: Foles lined up as a receiver and caught the TD.
funFact: Foles outdueled Tom Brady for the Eagles' first-ever Super Bowl. 🦅

question: Which Phillies closer was a perfect 48-for-48 in saves during the 2008 World Series championship season?
hint2: He got the final out of the 2008 World Series.
funFact: Lidge never blew a save all year and struck out Eric Hinske to clinch it all. 🔔

question: In 2010 the Flyers pulled off a rare comeback from a 3-0 series deficit, winning Game 7 against which Original Six rival?
hint1: An Original Six team from Massachusetts.
hint2: They share a bitter rivalry with Philly.
funFact: The Flyers won four straight, becoming just the third NHL team ever to erase a 3-0 series hole. 🟠

question: What's the only letter of the alphabet that doesn't appear in any U.S. state name?
funFact: Every other letter shows up in at least one state name — only Q is completely missing.

question: Sealed honey found in ancient Egyptian tombs was reportedly still edible after roughly how long?
funFact: Its low moisture and natural acidity make honey one of the only foods that essentially never goes bad.
